topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box sat on my worn kitchen table like a monument to forgotten obligations. A shipping label, scrawled in faded blue ink, bore the address of the previous resident—a ghost I’d never met. My fingers traced the rough edges of the packing tape, the cheap paper feeling abrasive against my skin. Every time I looked at it, a heavy, sinking feeling settled in my chest, a dull ache that made it hard to draw a full breath. I didn't know what was inside, but the anticipation only amplified the hollow space inside me. I pushed the box away with a sigh, the sound barely audible in the quiet apartment, and stared blankly at the peeling paint on the wall, wishing the floor would just swallow me whole.

[story 2]

When the delivery man handed me the parcel, his cheerful tone seemed jarringly loud in the silence of my cramped hallway. I took the package with a trembling hand, the weight of it seeming disproportionate to the meager contents I could already guess. Inside, there were old photographs and a stack of handwritten letters, relics of someone else’s life. A sudden wave of intense self-reproach washed over me. *Why me?* I thought, the question echoing in the cavern of my mind. I dropped the box onto the linoleum with a thud that seemed deafening. I spent the next hour just pacing the small space, my movements jerky and uncoordinated, unable to focus on anything other than the sheer, undeniable fact that I was just here, an accidental recipient of someone else's discarded history.

[story 3]

I kept the package tucked deep inside the closet, a dark, heavy secret I didn't want to acknowledge. The silence of the apartment was oppressive, magnifying the frantic drumming of my heart against my ribs. Every time I glanced toward the door, expecting someone—anyone—to interrupt the monotony, the realization hit me: I was fundamentally unremarkable. This package, addressed to a stranger, was just another piece of mail confirming my own invisibility. I picked up the box again, turning it over and over, the glossy surfaces mocking my own lack of substance. I dropped it again, the sound swallowed instantly by the stale air, and curled up on the sofa, pulling a thin blanket over my head, trying desperately to shrink into the shadows.
